turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in case 15-1189, Impression Products Incorporated versus Lexmark International Incorporated. This is a patent case from the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. Now, many patent cases are technical and difficult, but this one is actually pretty easy to understand. It involves patented toner cartridges for copier machines, but that is not important. The issue we address can come up with respect to any patented product. The Patent Act gives investors, known as patentees, the right to bring patent infringement lawsuits against people who make, use, sell, or import their patented products without getting permission to do so. But there is an important limit on this power. If the patentee sells a product, he loses the right to sue the purchaser under the patent laws for whatever the purchaser does with that item, most importantly, sell it to someone else. Now, if the purchaser does that, the new buyer gets the item free and clear of any patent claim. Now, of course, this does not include copying the item to make a new one; that is always within the control of the patentee. But as to the particular item sold, the patentee's patent rights are said to exhaust, which means the patentee is out of the picture as far as that item is concerned. That item passes into commerce free and clear. Now, in this case, the Federal Circuit concluded that exhaustion does not occur in two situations. First, if a patentee sells a product with an express limit on how owners may use or resell the item, the sale does not exhaust the patentee's rights. Patentees might for example, sell the item with the condition that the purchaser not resell it below a certain price. The Federal Circuit held that the patentee can sue for patent infringement if the purchaser or a subsequent owner violates that restriction. The second situation is when a patentee sells its products overseas. The Federal Circuit held that foreign sales do not exhaust patent rights, so if the patentee sells an item abroad, he can sue for infringement anyone who brings that product into the United States. We disagree with both of the federal circuit's conclusions. The exhaustion rule is much more clear-cut. Whenever and wherever a patentee sells an item, he loses the right to control that item through patent infringement lawsuits. Now, the basic idea behind exhaustion comes from a common law principle that has been well-established since the 17th Century, not just in patent law but generally. If a person sells a product, the purchaser and all subsequent owners should generally be free to use that item as they please. Permitting sellers to dictate how purchasers can use or sell a product would be a burden on commerce. The original seller might benefit from such an arrangement but the rest of the market would suffer. And so, the common law as it developed in England and came over here is hostile to any rule that allows sellers to restrain the free use of goods. This principle applies in the patent context. The Patent Act is designed to promote innovation by giving the patentee the right to stop others from making, using or selling its patented products. But once the patentee sells a product, probably at a high price, because he is the only one who can, he has received the special reward to which he is entitled. With that out of the way, there is no reason the patentee should keep the special patent law rights over the item he sold. Instead, the purchaser should be free to treat the product just like any other personal property allowing patentees to retain their patent rights after a sale would clog the free flow of commerce. Take a familiar example, a shop that restores and sells used cars. That business works because the owner knows that he is free to repair and resell those vehicles, replacing worn-out parts with substitutes he has from other used vehicles and so on. But if the companies that make car parts could keep their patent rights after the first sale, the shop owner would be in trouble. If any of those companies restricted the purchaser's resell rights, the shop owner could end up liable for infringement without even knowing it. Staying in business would be tough and buying a used car would be more expensive. Now, when Congress enacted the Patent Act, it was well aware of these practical problems and of the common law's disdain for restraints on the free flow of goods, what have been known for centuries as restraints on alienation. As a result, this Court has long held that when a patentee sells a product, its patent rights exhaust. Even if the patentee expressly tries to restrict how the purchaser can use that item, the patentee cannot enforce that restriction through a patent infringement lawsuit. The Federal Circuit's decision to the contrary is inconsistent with this settled case law. Now, the question whether a foreign sale triggers exhaustion is not so directly covered by our prior decisions but we think the answer should be the same, pretty much for the same reasons. When a patentee sells abroad, it obtains a reward for its invention, namely the price that the foreign purchaser pays for the item. It may not be as high as the price would be if the product were sold in the United States because United States patent rights do not apply abroad. But we do not think that is important. The Patent Act does not guarantee a particular price. Allowing the patentee to retain patent rights after a sale would however, seriously hamper international commerce. The patentee could lie in wait and sue for infringement whenever his product entered the United States. That threat of an infringement lawsuit would be a serious problem in today's interconnected world where the tiniest microchip can contain literally, thousands of patented elements drawn from complex global supply chains. In some patent exhaustion is a simple uniform rule. Whenever and wherever a patentee sells a product, that sale exhausts all of his rights in that item regardless of any restrictions that the patentee tries to impose or the location of the sale. That is what Congress intended. It is fair to the patentee and it preserves the free flow of commerce here and abroad. The judgment of the Federal Circuit is therefore reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Ginsburg has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
